content.thinking: Let me look at the chart image first to understand what we're working with.

I can see a chart showing "Nutrient Content in Animal Milk & Plant-Based Alternatives". The chart shows bubble sizes proportional to nutrient values for:
- Plant-based: Rice, Soy, Coconut, Oat, Almond
- Animal-based: Cow, Goat

Nutrients listed: Protein, Fat, Carbs, Calcium, Potassium, Sodium, Magnesium, Phosphorus, Sulfur, Iodine

Let me examine the chart more carefully. I need to identify which nutrient:
1. Is NOT well-supplied by plant-based products (small or no bubbles)


2. IS supplied by animal-based products (large bubbles)

Iodine appears to be the nutrient at the bottom of the chart, and it looks like it might show a stark difference between the two categories. Let me zoom in on that section to see the pattern more clearly.

input.description: Crop the bottom rows of the chart to see Sulfur and Iodine rows
Crop the very bottom rows showing Sulfur and Iodine

content.thinking:
Now I can clearly see:
- **Sulfur**: Has one bubble in the plant-based section (looks like it's in the Soy column based on position), and two large bubbles for the animal-based products (Cow and Goat)
- **Iodine**: Has NO bubbles in the plant-based section at all, but has two large bubbles for the animal-based products (Cow and Goat)

So Iodine is the nutrient that plant-based alternatives cannot supplement (no bubbles visible for any plant-based option), but is supplied by animal-based products (Cow and Goat show large bubbles).


That tracks with what I know about iodine—it's naturally scarce in plant foods unless they're grown in iodine-rich soil, and dairy is one of the most reliable sources since cows get iodine from their feed and it concentrates in milk.

content.text:
<think>
Looking at the Iodine row: there are **no bubbles at all** for any of the plant-based products (Rice, Soy, Coconut, Oat, Almond), while the animal-based products (Cow and Goat) show clearly large brown bubbles. 

For Sulfur, there is at least one plant-based bubble (appears to be Soy), so plant-based options can provide some sulfur.

Iodine is the nutrient with zero representation in all plant-based columns but substantial presence in the animal milk columns.
</think>

<answer>
**Iodine** — the Iodine row shows no bubbles whatsoever for any of the plant-based alternatives (Rice, Soy, Coconut, Oat, Almond), while both Cow and Goat milk display large bubbles, indicating significant iodine content. This makes it the nutrient you are most likely to be unable to supplement from the plant-based products shown.
</answer>
